data.title: 3 'Strong Buy' Stocks to Buy Now, 1/21/2026, According to Top Analysts
data.description:
Looking for top-rated stocks to buy now? Recent analyst updates highlight three companies that have earned Strong Buy ratings from Top Wall Street analysts. Each stock offers solid upside potential, supported by fresh Buy calls.

Claim 50% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

For more stock ideas, check out TipRanks' Analyst Top Stocks tool, which tracks the latest Buy ratings from top analysts in real time.

Here are today's top analyst-backed stock picks. Click on any ticker to research the company before deciding whether to add it to your portfolio.

Apellis Pharmaceuticals (APLS) – This is a biotech company that develops drugs for rare and serious immune-related diseases. Today, Bank of America Securities analyst Tazeen Ahmad maintained a Buy rating on the stock with a price target of $28 per share. Interestingly, nine out of the 11 Top Analysts who recently rated the stock gave it a Buy. Taken together, their 12-month price targets imply an upside of about 77.70%.

United Airlines Holdings (UAL) – United Airlines is a major U.S. airline that operates domestic and international passenger and cargo flights worldwide. Today, Barclays analyst Brandon Oglenski maintained a Buy rating on the stock with a price target of $150 per share. Interestingly, eight out of the nine Top Analysts who recently rated the stock gave it a Buy. Taken together, their 12-month price targets imply an upside of about 30.66%.

Aeva Technologies (AEVA) – Aeva Technologies develops advanced lidar sensors and software used in autonomous driving and industrial automation. Yesterday, Craig-Hallum analyst Richard Shannon maintained a Buy rating on the stock with a price target of $25 per share. Interestingly, four out of the five Top Analysts who recently rated the stock gave it a Buy. Taken together, their 12-month price targets imply an upside of about 22.14%.

Who Are the Top Analysts? 

TipRanks ranks financial analysts according to the success rates of their ratings and the average return on each of their ratings. The Top Analysts have each earned a five-star ranking, thanks to the accuracy and profitability of their ratings over time. 

See real-time analyst rankings and learn more about the performance of Top Analysts on TipRanks' Top Wall Street Analysts page.
data.author: Shalu Saraf

data.title: D-Wave (QBTS) Completes Quantum Circuits Deal as It Expands Beyond Annealing
data.description:
D-Wave Quantum (QBTS) announced it has completed its acquisition of Quantum Circuits Inc., adding a new type of quantum hardware to its business. As a result, D-Wave now offers both annealing and gate-based systems under a single company plan.

Claim 50% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

So far, D-Wave has focused on annealing machines that are already in use by paying customers. These systems are built to solve certain problems faster than classic computers, and some have been used in real-world research. Now, the company is adding a second approach that many experts see as key to long-term progress in quantum computing.

D-Wave said the goal is to serve a wider set of customers by offering two types of quantum tools instead of one. The company also said the two platforms are meant to work side by side over time.

Meanwhile, QBTS shares dropped 6.21% on Tuesday, closing at $27.04.
data.author: Ran Melamed

data.title: Stock Market News Today, 1/21/26 – U.S. Stock Futures Rise after S&P 500, Dow's Largest Drop Since October
data.description:
U.S. stock futures edged higher early Wednesday after the S&P 500 and the 30-stock Dow recorded their largest single-day drops since October 10. Traders are reacting to President Donald Trump's threat to escalate tariffs over his demand to acquire Greenland.

Claim 50% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

Futures on the Nasdaq 100 (NDX), the S&P 500 (SPX), and the Dow Jones Industrial Average (DJIA) were up 0.33%, 0.34%, and 0.21%, respectively, at 3:20 a.m. EST on January 21.

During Tuesday's regular trading session, the three major indexes closed sharply lower, with technology stocks leading the selloff. The Dow declined by 1.76%, the S&P 500 fell by 2.06%, and the Nasdaq Composite dropped 2.39%.

After the markets closed, Netflix (NFLX) stock fell more than 5%, despite beating sales and earnings expectations for Q4FY25 and reporting a solid surge in global paid subscribers to 325 million. Moreover, the streaming giant revised its bid for Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD) to an all-cash offer, fending off competition from Paramount Skydance (PSKY).

Looking forward, traders are focused on Trump's speech at the World Economic Forum in Davos today, where he will discuss the Greenland issue. Additionally, economic reports on housing starts, MBA mortgage applications, building permits, and pending home sales will be released today.

On the earnings front, companies reporting today include Johnson & Johnson (JNJ), Halliburton (HAL), and Charles Schwab (SCHW).

Notably, the U.S. 10-year Treasury yield was down, floating near 4.26%. WTI crude oil futures were trending lower, hovering near $59.63 per barrel as of the last check. Additionally, the Gold Spot U.S. dollar price increased to nearly $4,855 per ounce on Wednesday.

Elsewhere, European indexes opened lower today as traders anticipate Trump's speech in front of world leaders at Davos.

Asia-Pacific Markets Mixed

Asia-Pacific markets traded mixed on January 21 amid escalated geopolitical tensions involving Iran and Greenland.

Hong Kong's Hang Seng Index rose 0.36%. In China, the Shanghai Composite slipped 0.08%, while the Shenzhen Component gained 0.80%. Meanwhile, Japan's Nikkei dropped 0.41%, and the Topix declined 0.99%.

Stay ahead of macro events with our up-to-the-minute Economic Calendar — filter by impact, country, and more.
data.author: Sheryl Sheth

data.title: Goldman Sachs Sees a Tough Backdrop for IT Stocks, but Says You Can Still Win with These 3 'Strong Buy' Names
data.description:
Talk about tech these days, and most people will flash directly to AI. But Goldman Sachs analyst Katherine Murphy is digging deeper, uncovering opportunities across IT hardware. The premise is straightforward: hardware still matters, at every layer of the stack.

Claim 50% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

Like any industry, however, IT hardware is contending with real challenges. The sector is still digesting the aftereffects of the 2023 demand trough, when post-pandemic needs were largely pulled forward. Since then, higher tariffs and inflation-driven increases in input costs have weighed on performance. Adding to the pressure, expectations for PC demand this year have softened.

At the same time, where AI investment is taking place, it is beginning to provide tangible support. Rather than lifting the entire sector, that spending is flowing into specific areas of IT hardware where replacement cycles are still unfolding, creating selective opportunities even as investors continue to debate the durability of AI demand, the timing of broader refresh cycles, and the impact of higher costs on margins.

That selective dynamic underpins Murphy's outlook: "We are selectively positive on stocks that we view as best exposed to categories that still are in the expansion phase of their multi-year refresh cycle, specifically x86 servers, enterprise storage and campus networking… We prefer IT hardware vendors and distributors where we see upside to consensus estimates & which we believe screen attractively against three key investor debates into 2026: (1) How sustainable is AI demand?; (2) Where are we in the refresh cycle for PCs, servers, storage, and campus networking?; and (3) How will higher input costs impact margins and demand?"

Against this backdrop, Murphy pinpointed three IT stocks that could still deliver gains even in a challenging environment. And if you don't want to take her word for it, TipRanks data shows that all three carry Strong Buy consensus ratings. Let's dive in.

Dell Technologies (DELL)

The first Goldman pick we're looking at, Dell, is a well-known name from the PC world. The ~$80 billion company got its start back in 1994; in its modern incarnation, it is the result of the merger between Dell and EMC in 2016. Dell is an important supplier of PCs and laptops, as well as peripheral hardware – monitors, headsets, gaming accessories. In addition, the company also produces and distributes vital AI-capable hardware such as servers and memory storage.

On the AI side, Dell offers a wide portfolio of AI infrastructure products. As noted, these can include servers and memory storage; the company also produces hardware for AI networking, data management, and cyber resilience. The next generation of AI is going to be smaller and faster, and Dell is already producing AI-capable workstations, PCs, and laptops.

In an important – and related – announcement, Dell made public on January 6 this year the release of its new XPS laptops. This is a reestablishment of one of the company's iconic products, and the new XPS 14 and XPS 16 are already available. The gaming version features Intel's Core Ultra 200HX processors, while the flagship model has AMD's 2nd Gen Ryze 9000X3D chip. The new XPS series is AI-capable, and the XPS-13 is due out later this year. Dell has also recently released new monitors, including the first 52-inch 6K monitor to hit the markets, and a 32-inch 4K QD-OLED monitor.

At the end of this past November, Dell released its results for fiscal 3Q26 – and in that quarter the company brought in $27 billion in revenue, a third quarter record that was up 11% year-over-year – but missed the forecast by $296 million. At the bottom line, Dell's non-GAAP diluted EPS grew 17% year-over-year to reach $2.59, beating the forecast by 11 cents per share.

Katherine Murphy, in her write-up for Goldman, says of Dell: "We believe that DELL will continue to be a leader in the AI server OEM market for neocloud customers, supported by the company's differentiated engineering expertise & services capabilities. We see upside to DELL's outlook for AI server revenue to grow at a 20-25% CAGR (F2026-30) at mid single digit% EBIT margins from (1) upward capex revisions from tier two cloud services providers; and (2) more material demand from sovereign & enterprise customers for AI infrastructure, which should be margin accretive."

Looking ahead, Murphy explains why she believes the stock should post gains this year, writing, "While outlook for growth in traditional servers and storage is more modest (mid single digit % growth in core ISG) and PCs demand should be challenged by higher pricing, we are encouraged by DELL's self-help actions to gain profitable share, which should serve to partially offset any consolidated margin pressure from greater AI server mix or increased commodity costs."

Murphy follows these comments with a Buy rating and a 12-month price target of $165, implying an upside of 48.5% by early 2027.

Overall, Dell's Strong Buy consensus rating is based on 16 analyst reviews that break down to 13 Buys, 2 Holds, and 1 Sell. The stock's $111.07 trading price and $164 average target price together indicate a 48% gain in store for the year ahead. (See DELL stock forecast)
data.author: Michael Marcus

data.title: TSLA, BYD Face Memory Chip Shortage in 2026, Wells Fargo Warns
data.description:
Analysts at Wells Fargo, one of Wall Street's leading research firms, released a note stating that automakers could face renewed cost pressures and potential supply interruptions as a widening global memory-chip shortage intensifies, spurred by surging demand from data centers and AI workloads.

Claim 50% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

The firm noted that the top 10 automotive Tier 1 suppliers consumed about 54% of total DRAM (Dynamic Random Access Memory) in 2025, led by giants like BYD (BYDDF), Tesla (TSLA), and Aptiv (APTV). Yet autos account for under 10% of the global DRAM market, leaving the sector at a disadvantage as chipmakers prioritize higher-margin clients in cloud computing and AI.

Tight Parts Supply Could Raise Auto Prices

DRAM is widely used for vehicle infotainment, advanced driver-assistance systems (ADAS), and centralized electronic architectures. Its prices have skyrocketed lately, stripping carmakers of bargaining leverage. Wells Fargo noted that DDR5 spot prices have surged over eight times and DDR4 more than sixteen times above 2024 averages.

Analysts estimate current DRAM content at $50 to $110 per vehicle, cautioning that these hikes could deliver a hefty cost hit to the auto sector in 2026. They noted that global DRAM demand is expected to climb by about 26% next year, outpacing supply growth of roughly 21%.

Micron (MU) remains the largest supplier to the auto sector, along with Samsung (SSNLF) and SK Hynix. These chipmakers are allocating greater time and resources to build the faster growing and more profitable data center markets than the auto sector.

Consequently, automakers and suppliers may face steeper prices to secure supply, a risk Wells Fargo warns could squeeze margins and disrupt production schedules. Analysts added that premium vehicle segments and EV makers appear most vulnerable due to their elevated memory usage per vehicle.

Wells Fargo already sees emerging signs of panic buying similar to that of the 2021 chip crisis, warning that failure to absorb higher costs could trigger production halts.

Tesla or BYD: Which Is the Better Auto Stock?

A tightening memory market could raise auto costs and pressure margins, particularly for memory-heavy EVs like Tesla and BYD.

We used the TipRanks Stock Comparison Tool to determine which stock between Tesla and BYD is currently favored by analysts. Notably, BYDDF stock has a "Strong Buy," while Tesla has a "Hold" consensus, suggesting analysts remain highly optimistic about BYD's long-term prospects.
data.author: Sheryl Sheth

data.title: Why Stifel Turns More Bullish on Ondas Stock (ONDS) After Investor Day Update
data.description:
Ondas Holdings (ONDS) drew fresh support from Wall Street after hosting a recent virtual investor day, where the company laid out its plans for its drone and autonomous systems business. The company raised its 2026 revenue target to $170 million–$180 million, citing a growing pipeline and improving visibility. Following the event, top Stifel analyst Jonathan Siegmann raised his price target on Ondas to $18 from $17 and kept a Buy rating on the stock.

Claim 50% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

Investor Day Strengthens the Bull Case

Siegmann said the investor day increased his confidence in Ondas' long-term story, especially its position in the military drone market. He noted that the company's revenue and growth targets were well above his earlier estimates and showed clearer progress across the business.

He added that the presentation also gave him a better understanding of how Ondas stands out in the defense drone space, where demand continues to rise.

At the same time, Siegmann acknowledged that Ondas is still early in building its business. Even so, he pointed to the speed of recent progress as a positive sign. Over the past six months, the company has raised about $1.8 billion, acquired new technology, partnered with major contract manufacturers, and strengthened its leadership team.

Overall, Siegmann believes these steps are helping Ondas move faster and build a stronger base for growth. As a result, he expects 2026 to be an important year for the company and sees potential for the stock to perform well as execution continues.

Is Ondas Holdings a Good Stock to Buy?  

Currently, Wall Street has a Strong Buy consensus rating on Ondas stock based on eight unanimous Buy recommendations. The average ONDS stock price target of $16.63 indicates about 26.66% upside potential.
data.author: Shalu Saraf
